headline: POCA Allows 2-Year Renewable Detention With No Judicial Review — Decided by an Appointed Board
context: The Prevention of Crime Act 1959, amended in 2014, lets an appointed Prevention Board impose 2-year detention orders renewable indefinitely. The 2014 amendment removed judicial review of Board decisions.

cards.big: POCA detention orders run 2 years and renew indefinitely — and the 2014 amendment removed judicial review of the appointed Board's decisions.
cards.sub: Detainees enter via 60-day police investigation periods (24h initial + 21-day + 38-day extensions) before the Prevention Board hearing.

cards.big: POCA 2014 amendment removed judicial review of Prevention Board decisions — the Board's reasoning is not subject to High Court scrutiny.
cards.sub: Board members are appointed by the Yang di-Pertuan Agong on the minister's advice. There is no public reasoning standard.

cards.big: Article 5 protects personal liberty — POCA operates as a constitutional carve-out under Article 149.
cards.sub: Article 149 specifically enables Parliament to enact preventive-detention laws notwithstanding other liberty protections.

cards.big: Civil-society reports indicate POCA detainees are disproportionately drawn from lower-income communities — though disaggregated official data is not published.
cards.sub: The asymmetry between civil-society reporting and official disclosure is itself a structural transparency gap.

cards.big: POCA is not an emergency power that has been reluctantly retained — it is an active, routine detention regime, just one without a courtroom.
Like a prison sentence decided by an appointed committee — same outcome, no courtroom, no published reasoning.
cards.sub: Preventive detention achieves imprisonment without the constitutional safeguards of a criminal trial.

cards.big: POCA reform requires either restoring judicial review of Board decisions or publishing disaggregated outcome data — preferably both.
